questions.prompt: Users complain your assistant has 'too many prompts.' Audit shows 80% of approvals are on actions they always approve. Right move?

choices.text: Tiered approvals: drafts auto-allowed (reversible); sends always prompted (irreversible); financial actions always two-person. Plus user-opt-in rule-based auto-approvals for narrow patterns (e.g. 'auto-acknowledge family pings'). Friction drops; safety on the actions that matter holds.

questions.explanation: Approval everywhere creates rubber-stamping. Tier by reversibility: reversible auto-allowed, irreversible always-prompted, destructive/financial gated harder. User-opted-in rules let common low-stakes patterns flow. Meaningful gates, not gates everywhere.

questions.prompt: When an assistant's notification-approval prompt times out, what should happen?

choices.text: Default to skip and notify the user it was skipped. Silence isn't consent. Auto-executing on timeout would mean any action the user didn't see would go through — exactly the wrong default for irreversible operations.
Send the user a reminder forever.

questions.explanation: Notifications can fail (the user never sees them, they were asleep, on vacation, etc.). Defaulting to action on silence assumes consent the user never gave. Default to skip + audit; user can re-trigger if they wanted it.
